APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work directly targets semi-supervised sequence tagging (NER and chunking) using pretrained bidirectional LMs and reports state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003, CoNLL 2000) — clearly within top-tier NLP/ML venue scope. The methodology, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics (mean F1, significance testing) match community expectations and will interest the venue's audience. The paper is well positioned in the literature and addresses a timely direction (large-scale LM pretraining for structured prediction). | B(5): Yes — the paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML conference: it tackles semi-supervised sequence tagging (NER and chunking) using pretrained bidirectional language model embeddings (TagLM) and reports new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 (91.93 F1) and CoNLL 2000 (96.37 F1). The methodology (pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark, integrating LM embeddings into a hierarchical BiRNN+CRF tagger) and evaluations (mean F1, stddev, significance testing, comparisons to strong baselines like Lample et al., Ma and Hovy, Chiu and Nichols) match top-venue standards. The problem formulation, audience, and positioning within the NLP/ML community are appropriate and well supported by the experiments and related-work coverage.

The paper is well positioned in the literature and addresses a timely direction (large-scale LM pretraining for structured prediction).

The problem formulation, audience, and positioning within the NLP/ML community are appropriate and well supported by the experiments and related-work coverage.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and presents a clear logical flow from method (pretraining bidirectional LMs and extracting embeddings) to experiments (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) and conclusions; the main contributions are stated early and supported by concrete results (mean F1s, p-values). Notation and experimental details appear adequately reported (hyperparameters reported, related work coverage thorough), and writing quality is described as good. Minor presentation issues remain—e.g., test-set results were withheld for anonymity and the heavy computational cost of LM training is noted—which slightly reduces clarity for a reader seeking final results or reproducibility details. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and follows a clear flow from introduction through methods and experiments, with main contributions (TagLM and state-of-the-art results on CoNLL NER/Chunking) stated early. Notation and experimental details appear well-documented (hyperparameters reported, LM perplexities and F1 means/SDs provided), and figures/tables/examples seem effective given the thorough reporting of baselines and statistical significance. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is thorough, though a few minor presentation issues remain (e.g., computational-cost caveats and withheld test set details) that prevent an exceptional rating.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable novel technique: incorporating pretrained bidirectional language model embeddings (concatenating top-layer forward and backward LM token representations) as additional input features to supervised sequence taggers, which goes beyond prior use of static pretrained word embeddings. Sequence tagging itself is well-studied, but the specific method of extracting and integrating bidirectional LM embeddings and demonstrating consistent SOTA gains (e.g., 91.93 F1 on CoNLL 2003 NER) distinguishes it from prior work. The authors clearly differentiate their approach from existing baselines and show non-trivial empirical benefits, particularly in low-resource settings, so the contribution is more than incremental without being a radical paradigm shift. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly defined new method (TagLM) that augments supervised sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings—an approach that differs from prior sequence tagging and transfer methods listed (e.g., Lample et al., Ma and Hovy, Collobert et al.). It demonstrates non-trivial technical development (pretraining forward and backward LMs on the 1B Word Benchmark and concatenating top-layer LM embeddings into a hierarchical tagger) and achieves new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking, indicating a notable novelty beyond incremental combination of known techniques. The paper also distinguishes its contribution through empirical comparisons showing larger gains than other transfer/joint learning approaches and clear claims about when the method is most helpful (low-resource settings).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable novel technique: incorporating pretrained bidirectional language model embeddings (concatenating top-layer forward and backward LM token representations) as additional input features to supervised sequence taggers, which goes beyond prior use of static pretrained word embeddings. Sequence tagging itself is well-studied, but the specific method of extracting and integrating bidirectional LM embeddings and demonstrating consistent SOTA gains (e.g., 91.93 F1 on CoNLL 2003 NER) distinguishes it from prior work. The authors clearly differentiate their approach from existing baselines and show non-trivial empirical benefits, particularly in low-resource settings, so the contribution is more than incremental without being a radical paradigm shift.

It demonstrates non-trivial technical development (pretraining forward and backward LMs on the 1B Word Benchmark and concatenating top-layer LM embeddings into a hierarchical tagger) and achieves new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking, indicating a notable novelty beyond incremental combination of known techniques. The paper also distinguishes its contribution through empirical comparisons showing larger gains than other transfer/joint learning approaches and clear claims about when the method is most helpful (low-resource settings).

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper tackles an important, practical problem—improving sequence tagging via semi‑supervised pretrained LMs—and demonstrates clear empirical gains: new state‑of‑the‑art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (mean F1 91.93) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37) and average absolute improvements of ~1.06 F1 (NER) and ~1.37 F1 (Chunking) over a strong baseline. The approach uses large‑scale LM pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark and yields especially large relative gains in low‑resource settings, so it is likely to change how researchers and practitioners incorporate unlabeled data and be widely adopted and extended. While training large LMs is computationally expensive, the demonstrated SOTA results and applicability across core tagging tasks imply broad impact beyond this subfield. | B(5): The paper demonstrates a clearly significant practical effect: augmenting taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings yields new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (91.93 mean F1) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37 mean F1) and provides average absolute gains of 1.06 F1 (NER) and 1.37 F1 (Chunking) over strong baselines. It also often outperforms other transfer/joint-learning approaches and shows especially large relative gains in low-resource settings (e.g., when training data is reduced to 1%), indicating broad utility. Given these results and the generality of using bidirectional LM embeddings as features, the contribution is likely to substantially influence subsequent research and practice in NLP.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—improving sequence tagging via semi‑supervised pretrained LMs—and demonstrates clear empirical gains: new state‑of‑the‑art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (mean F1 91.93) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37) and average absolute improvements of ~1.06 F1 (NER) and ~1.37 F1 (Chunking) over a strong baseline. The approach uses large‑scale LM pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark and yields especially large relative gains in low‑resource settings, so it is likely to change how researchers and practitioners incorporate unlabeled data and be widely adopted and extended. While training large LMs is computationally expensive, the demonstrated SOTA results and applicability across core tagging tasks imply broad impact beyond this subfield.

It also often outperforms other transfer/joint-learning approaches and shows especially large relative gains in low-resource settings (e.g., when training data is reduced to 1%), indicating broad utility. Given these results and the generality of using bidirectional LM embeddings as features, the contribution is likely to substantially influence subsequent research and practice in NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are strong: evaluations on two standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking), comparisons to many prior systems, reporting of mean F1 with significance tests, LM perplexities, and low-resource (1%) experiments support the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is present to an extent (e.g., low-resource behavior, hyperparameters reported), but the summary does not indicate thorough ablations on LM design choices (layers, sizes) or more exhaustive sensitivity studies. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations are acknowledged (computational cost of LM pretraining, explicit exceptions), but reproducibility may be hindered by large compute needs and no mention of released code. Overall this is solid and comprehensive with minor gaps, so a 4 is appropriate. | B(4): The experiments are solid and focused: the paper evaluates on two standard sequence-tagging tasks (CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking), pretrains LMs on a large unlabeled corpus (1B Word Benchmark), compares to many strong baselines, reports mean F1 with standard deviation and significance tests, and includes low-resource (1% data) experiments showing sensitivity. Ablation/sensitivity-style analyses appear to be present (comparisons to other transfer approaches and low-data experiments), and hyperparameters are reported to aid reproduction. Minor gaps remain around computational cost and reproducibility (large LM training expense, code not mentioned) and uncertainty about whether other sequence-tagging model choices would change results, so I rate it a 4 rather than a 5.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are strong: evaluations on two standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 chunking), comparisons to many prior systems, reporting of mean F1 with significance tests, LM perplexities, and low-resource (1%) experiments support the main claims. Ablation/sensitivity analysis is present to an extent (e.g., low-resource behavior, hyperparameters reported), but the summary does not indicate thorough ablations on LM design choices (layers, sizes) or more exhaustive sensitivity studies. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and limitations are acknowledged (computational cost of LM pretraining, explicit exceptions), but reproducibility may be hindered by large compute needs and no mention of released code. Overall this is solid and comprehensive with minor gaps, so a 4 is appropriate.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are clearly stated and supported by controlled experiments: pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (from the 1B Word Benchmark) added to a hierarchical neural tagger yield measured F1 improvements on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking, with comparisons to appropriate baselines and reported p-values and hyperparameters. There are no mathematical claims requiring proofs and the experimental methodology appears sound (proper baselines, statistical testing). Minor concerns remain — the authors note withheld test-set reporting for anonymity and the heavy computational cost of training large LMs — which limit immediate independent verification of the stated state-of-the-art but do not undermine the experimental evidence presented. Overall the paper is technically solid with only minor issues that do not affect the main conclusions. | B(5): The main claims are directly supported by experiments: TagLM obtains higher mean F1 on CoNLL 2003 NER (91.93) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37) using bidirectional LM embeddings pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark. The paper compares against strong, appropriate baselines (several recent neural taggers), reports hyperparameters and statistical significance testing, and documents limitations (compute cost, held-back test set) transparently. No mathematical derivations were required beyond model descriptions, and I see no unsupported assumptions or logical gaps in the summary that would undermine the conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by controlled experiments: pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (from the 1B Word Benchmark) added to a hierarchical neural tagger yield measured F1 improvements on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking, with comparisons to appropriate baselines and reported p-values and hyperparameters. There are no mathematical claims requiring proofs and the experimental methodology appears sound (proper baselines, statistical testing). Minor concerns remain — the authors note withheld test-set reporting for anonymity and the heavy computational cost of training large LMs — which limit immediate independent verification of the stated state-of-the-art but do not undermine the experimental evidence presented. Overall the paper is technically solid with only minor issues that do not affect the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against multiple recent and relevant baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Collobert et al. 2011) and reports results on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using the same F1 metric. Comparisons appear fair: mean F1, standard deviations, and statistical significance (p-values) are reported, and a strong baseline hierarchical tagger similar to prior work is used. The paper analyzes differences (absolute F1 gains, behavior in low-resource settings, comparison to other transfer/joint learning approaches) and provides thorough related-work coverage. Given these points, comparisons are comprehensive and fair. | B(5): The paper compares to a wide range of appropriate and recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Collobert et al. 2011) on the standard CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 chunking datasets using the same F1 metric and statistical testing. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), include ablations and low-resource experiments showing when TagLM helps most, and the related work coverage is described as thorough. The paper reports when prior approaches achieve comparable results and discusses limitations (e.g., computational cost of LM pretraining), indicating clear analysis of differences with competing methods.

main_claims: Introducing TagLM: augmenting supervised sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional language model (LM) embeddings improves performance on sequence tagging tasks.
TagLM establishes new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (91.93 mean F1) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37 mean F1) without using additional labeled data or task-specific gazetteers.
Using bidirectional LM embeddings yields average absolute improvements of 1.06 F1 for NER and 1.37 F1 for Chunking over the baseline.
TagLM often provides larger gains than other forms of transfer or joint learning and is especially helpful in low-resource settings (e.g., bigger relative gains when training data is reduced to 1%).
_summary.methodology: Pre-train forward and backward neural language models on large unlabeled corpora (1B Word Benchmark), extract top-layer forward and backward LM embeddings for each token, concatenate them to form bidirectional LM embeddings, and include these embeddings as additional input features to a supervised hierarchical neural sequence tagger (token char encoder + bidirectional RNN + CRF).
baselines: Baseline hierarchical neural sequence tagger (similar to Ma and Hovy, 2016; Lample et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2017; Chiu and Nichols, 2016)
metrics: Statistical significance testing (p-values reported for comparisons)
limitations: LM embeddings cannot be directly combined with expectation maximization in the manner described (explicit exception noted).
Test set performance was withheld to preserve anonymity at the time of the manuscript and will be updated before publication.
Training large LMs can be computationally expensive (e.g., the CNN-BIG-LSTM referenced took three weeks to train on 32 GPUs).
It is unclear whether other (non-discriminative) sequence tagging model complexities would be sufficient to effectively make use of LM embeddings.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semi-supervised sequence tagging (named entity recognition and chunking)
